summary: Jeff Atwood tackles the perennial challenge of software estimation, introducing Planning Poker as an accessible technique while arguing that historical data is ultimately superior. He explains how Planning Poker derives from the 1968 Wideband Delphi method and uses group consensus to improve estimates. However, Atwood's main thesis is that real estimation improvement comes from collecting and analyzing historical data about past estimates versus actual completion times. He highlights FogBugz's evidence-based scheduling as a practical implementation, showing how it can predict ship dates and identify which developers estimate accurately. The post concludes that without institutional memory of past estimates, teams are doomed to repeat the same estimation mistakes indefinitely.
key_points: Planning Poker is a fun, accessible technique based on the 1968 Wideband Delphi method that uses group consensus and discussion to converge on estimates
The best estimates come from historical data, not gut feelings or group games - you should start collecting estimation data immediately if you haven't already
Evidence-based scheduling tools can predict ship dates with probability ranges, identify developers on the critical path, and show how accurate individual estimators really are
Collecting good historical data requires discipline and team buy-in to time tracking, which isn't trivial
Without institutional estimation memory, teams will keep repeating the same estimation errors over and over
money_quotes: How'd you do? If you're like the rest of us, you suck. At estimating, I mean.
If anything says 'we have no clue how long this will take,' it's naming your estimation process after ancient, gas-huffing priestesses who offered advice in the form of cryptic riddles.
Like poker, the stakes in software development are real money – although we're usually playing with someone else's money.
The best estimates you can possibly produce are those based on historical data.
The dirty little secret of evidence based scheduling is that collecting this kind of historical data isn't trivial. Garbage in, garbage out.
Historical estimate data is fundamental to the science of software engineering.
Without some kind of institutional estimation memory – without appreciating the power of historical data – you're likely to keep repeating the same estimation errors over and over.
key_insight: While Planning Poker is a useful starting point for software estimation, the real path to accurate predictions is systematically collecting historical data about your estimates versus actual outcomes, building institutional memory that prevents repeating the same mistakes.
